---
date: 2026-06-14
title: Friedrich Nietzsche - Voice Extraction
source: Published works, correspondence, Ecce Homo, Nachlass
---

# Friedrich Nietzsche - Voice Extraction

## Verbatim Quotes

| Quote | Source | Date | Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| "What does not kill me makes me stronger." | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #8 | 1888 | Aphoristic declaration on suffering as growth |
| "Without music, life would be a mistake." | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #33 | 1888 | Statement on music as existential necessity |
| "God is dead. God remains dead. And we have killed him." | The Gay Science, §125 | 1882 | The Madman in the marketplace |
| "He who fights with monsters might take care lest he also become a monster. And when you gaze long into an abyss the abyss also gazes into you." | Beyond Good and Evil, §146 | 1886 | Warning about the dangers of combat with evil |
| "Whatever is done for love always occurs beyond good and evil." | Beyond Good and Evil, §153 | 1886 | Maxim on love transcending morality |
| "One must shed the bad taste of wanting to agree with many. 'Good' is no longer good when one's neighbor mouths it." | Beyond Good and Evil, §43 | 1886 | Aristocratic rejection of consensus |
| "I am no man, I am dynamite." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Fatality" | 1888 | Self-characterization as explosive force |
| "My formula for greatness in man is amor fati: the fact that a man wishes nothing to be different, either in front of him or behind him, or for all eternity." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §10 | 1888 | Definitive statement of his central doctrine |
| "I am a disciple of the philosopher Dionysus, and I would prefer to be even a satyr than a saint." | Ecce Homo, Preface §2 | 1888 | Self-identification with Dionysian philosophy |
| "Listen! for I am such and such a person. For Heaven's sake do not confound me with any one else!" | Ecce Homo, Preface §1 | 1888 | Demand for recognition, fear of being misunderstood |
| "How much truth can a certain mind endure; how much truth can it dare? - these questions became for me ever more and more the actual test of values." | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 | Truth as courage, as measure of rank |
| "I teach you the Superman. Man is something that should be overcome. What have you done to overcome him?" | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §3 | 1883 | Zarathustra's opening proclamation |
| "I entreat you, my brothers, remain true to the earth, and do not believe those who speak to you of superterrestrial hopes! They are poisoners, whether they know it or not." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue §3 | 1883 | Anti-metaphysical exhortation |
| "Now I bid you lose me and find yourselves; and only when ye have all denied me will I come back unto you." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "The Bestowing Virtue" | 1883 | Anti-discipleship, demand for autonomy |
| "The secret for harvesting from existence the greatest fruitfulness and the greatest enjoyment is: to live dangerously! Build your cities on the slopes of Vesuvius!" | The Gay Science, §283 | 1882 | Call to embrace risk and vitality |
| "I condemn Christianity; I bring against the Christian church the most terrible of all the accusations that an accuser has ever had in his mouth." | The Antichrist, §62 | 1888 | Final polemical declaration |
| "I call Christianity the one great curse, the one great intrinsic depravity, the one great instinct of revenge, for which no means are venomous enough, or secret, subterranean and small enough - I call it the one immortal blemish upon the human race." | The Antichrist, §62 | 1888 | Culminating condemnation |
| "The Christian church has left nothing untouched by its depravity; it has turned every value into worthlessness, and every truth into a lie, and every integrity into baseness of soul." | The Antichrist, §62 | 1888 | Systematic denunciation |
| "Supposing that Truth is a woman - what then? Is there not ground for suspecting that all philosophers, in so far as they have been dogmatists, have failed to understand women?" | Beyond Good and Evil, Preface | 1886 | Provocative opening gambit |
| "It is a mere prejudice that I am a human being. Yet I have often enough dwelled among human beings and I know the things human beings experience, from the lowest to the highest." | Letter to Cosima Wagner | January 3, 1889 | Madness letter, grandiose self-identification |
| "What is unpleasant and a strain on my modesty is that in fact I am every name in history." | Letter to Jacob Burckhardt | January 6, 1889 | Madness letter, dissolution of identity |
| "I go everywhere in my student coat, now and then slap someone on the back, and say: siamo contenti? son dio, ho fatto questa caricatura." | Letter to Jacob Burckhardt | January 6, 1889 | Madness letter - "Is everything OK? I am God, this farce is my creation" |
| "I have truly not become a 'monster'!" | Letter to Franz Overbeck | 1882 | Defensive plea to friend about his isolation |
| "So! And now candor 'unto death'! My dear friend! I am bound by nothing and change my plans with the greatest ease if you have plans." | Letter to Lou von Salome | May 1882 | Intimate, eager proposal of intellectual partnership |
| "I am now seeking people who could be my heirs; I carry some things around with me that absolutely cannot be read in my books - and am seeking for this the most beautiful and fruitful farmland." | Letter to Lou von Salome | May 1882 | Revealing vulnerability, intellectual loneliness |
| "Meine liebe Lou, Ihr Gedanke einer Reduktion der philosophischen Systeme auf Personal-Akten ihrer Urheber ist recht ein Gedanke aus dem 'Geschwistergehirn'" | Letter to Lou von Salome | September 16, 1882 | "Your thought of reducing philosophical systems to the personal acts of their authors is truly a thought from our 'sibling brain'" |
| "Dies System ist widerlegt und tot - aber die Person dahinter ist unwiderlegbar, die Person ist gar nicht tot zu machen." | Letter to Lou von Salome | September 16, 1882 | "This system is refuted and dead - but the person behind it is irrefutable, the person simply cannot be killed" |
| "I myself am currently working on a pro memoria for the courts of Europe for the purpose of an anti-German league. I want to bind the 'Reich' in an iron shirt and provoke a war of desperation." | Letter to Franz Overbeck | December 26, 1888 | Megalomaniac pre-collapse letter |
| "Although I have been prevented from becoming an officer of the Reserve, I shall contrive slowly and gradually to vanish from the list of those liable to serve." | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | August 1868 | Matter-of-fact military avoidance |
| "As regards the conditions of culture in the immediate future I feel the deepest misgivings. If only we are not forced to pay too dearly for this huge national success in a quarter where I at least refuse to suffer any loss." | Letter (quoted in Selected Letters preface) | November 7, 1870 | Anti-Prussian critique during Franco-Prussian War |
| "German politics are only another form of permanent winter and bad weather." | Letter from Nice | February 24, 1887 | Political disgust at German Empire |
| "Germany for the last 15 years has become a regular school of besotment. Water, rubbish and filth, far and wide that is what it looks like from a distance." | Letter from Nice | February 24, 1887 | Scathing cultural critique of Germany |
| "I forgive no one for compromising with it in any way, even if his name be Richard Wagner." | Letter from Nice | February 24, 1887 | Uncompromising intellectual standard |
| "My dear mother: I have received everything in the way of food and necessaries in life unfortunately, too, your letter, which made me feel very wretched." | Letter to his mother | c. 1880s | Pained response to Christian proselytizing |
| "Really these dissertations on Christianity and on opinions of this man and that as to what I should do and ought to think on the subject should no longer be directed to my address. My patience won't stand it!" | Letter to his mother | c. 1880s | Direct, exasperated boundary-setting |
| "If people of this kind, even including my mother and sister, had an inkling of what I am aiming at, they would have no alternative but to become my natural enemies." | Letter to his mother | c. 1880s | Acknowledging his radical isolation |
| "It spoils my love of life to live among such people, and I have to exercise considerable self-control in order to not react constantly against sanctimonious atmosphere." | Letter to his mother | c. 1880s | Personal suffering among believers |
| "I am a bombardier in the second mounted division of the Fourth Horse Artillery." | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | December 1, 1867 | Blunt, amused announcement of military service |
| "Believe me, old man, I never thought I should have an opportunity of growing vain about this sort of thing." | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | February 1868 | Self-deprecating humor about riding skill |
| "Ah, my dear friend, what a child of misfortune is a field artilleryman when he has literary tastes into the bargain." | Letter to Rohde | February 1868 | Comic self-pity, mixing registers |
| "Our old god of War loved young women, not shrivelled old Muses." | Letter to Rohde | February 1868 | Playful mythological quip |
| "A gunner who often enough in his barrack room sits upon a dirty stool meditating upon Democritean problems, while his boots are being polished for him, is really a paradox on whom the gods must look with scorn." | Letter to Rohde | February 1868 | Self-mocking comic image |
| "Dear Mother: I am very sorry that I was not able to meet you at Almrich yesterday, but I was prevented from coming by being kept in. And thereby hangs a tale which I will tell you." | Letter to his mother | November 1862 | Teenager's confessional, narrative setup |
| "The stern masters, who were very much surprised that anyone should introduce humour into so solemn an undertaking, summoned me to attend the Synod." | Letter to his mother | November 1862 | Young Nietzsche's comic subversion of authority |
| "I have not yet played with the soldiers, but will do so soon." | Letter to his sister | March 1856 | 11-year-old Fritz writing to Elizabeth |
| "It is Saturday, and the day too is drawing to a close. For a soldier the word 'Saturday' is full of magic charm and of a feeling of quiet and peace of which as a student I had no idea." | Letter to Rohde | February 1868 | Lyrical contentment in military routine |
| "If we possess our why of life we can put up with almost any how." | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #12 | 1888 | Aphorism on meaning and endurance |
| "Man does not aspire to happiness; only the Englishman does that." | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #12 | 1888 | Anti-utilitarian jab at English philosophy |
| "Idleness is the parent of all psychology. What? Is psychology then a - vice?" | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #1 | 1888 | Self-referential joke about his own occupation |
| "Which is it? Is man only a blunder of God? Or is God only a blunder of man?" | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #7 | 1888 | Reversible aphorism, theological provocation |
| "Aristotle says that in order to live alone, a man must be either an animal or a god. The third alternative is lacking: a man must be both - a philosopher." | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #3 | 1888 | Self-identification through classical reference |
| "'All truth is simple.' - Is not this a double lie?" | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #4 | 1888 | Meta-skepticism, self-undermining logic |
| "What does it matter if I am right? I am much too right. And he who laughs best today will also laugh last." | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #43 | 1888 | Triumphant, prophetic confidence |
| "When stepped on, a worm doubles up. That is clever. In that way he lessens the probability of being stepped on again. In the language of morality: humility." | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims and Arrows #31 | 1888 | Satirical reframing of virtue as survival mechanism |
| "To recognize untruth as a condition of life - that certainly means resisting accustomed value feelings in a dangerous way." | Beyond Good and Evil, §4 | 1886 | Radical epistemological claim |
| "In the end things must be as they are and have always been - the great things remain for the great, the abysses for the profound, the delicacies and thrills for the refined, and, to sum up shortly, everything rare for the rare." | Beyond Good and Evil, §43 | 1886 | Aristocratic fatalism |
| "Swallow your poison, for you need it badly." | Beyond Good and Evil | 1886 | Imperative addressed to the weak |
| "'Knowledge for its own sake' - this is the last snare set by morality: one therewith gets completely entangled with it once more." | Beyond Good and Evil, §64 | 1886 | Suspicion of disinterested knowledge |
| "The charm of knowledge would be small if so much shame did not have to be overcome on the road to it." | Beyond Good and Evil, §65 | 1886 | Psychological insight on the thrill of taboo |
| "Love of one is a piece of barbarism: for it is practised at the expense of all others. Love of God likewise." | Beyond Good and Evil, §67 | 1886 | Aphorism linking exclusive love to cruelty |
| "A thing explained is a thing we have no further concern with." | Beyond Good and Evil, §80 | 1886 | On the deadening effect of explanation |
| "Around the hero everything becomes a tragedy, around the demigod a satyr-play; and around God - what? perhaps a 'world'?" | Beyond Good and Evil, §150 | 1886 | Cosmological humor, ascending comedy |
| "One has to requite good and ill: but why to precisely the person who did us good or ill?" | Beyond Good and Evil, §71 | 1886 | Questioning justice at its root |
| "I no longer know, my dear fellow-man and neighbour, if thou canst at all live to the disadvantage of the race." | The Gay Science, §1 | 1882 | Ironic address, undermining moral certainty |
| "To laugh at oneself as one would have to laugh in order to laugh out of the veriest truth - to do this, the best have not yet had enough of the sense of truth." | The Gay Science, §1 | 1882 | Laughter as philosophical achievement |
| "In me the Christianity of my forbears reaches its logical conclusion. In me the stern intellectual conscience that Christianity fosters and makes paramount turns against Christianity. In me Christianity devours itself." | The Antichrist (Introduction/Mencken) | 1888 | Self as culmination of Christian self-overcoming |
| "On this perfect day, when everything is ripening, and not only the grapes are getting brown, a ray of sunshine has fallen on my life: I looked behind me, I looked before me, and never have I seen so many good things all at once." | Ecce Homo, Preface (opening) | 1888 | Birthday reflection, harvest metaphor |
| "Not in vain have I buried my four-and-fortieth year to-day; I had the right to bury it - that in it which still had life, has been saved and is immortal." | Ecce Homo, Preface | 1888 | Self-mythologizing birthday statement |
| "How could I help being thankful to the whole of my life? That is why I am now going to tell myself the story of my life." | Ecce Homo, Preface | 1888 | Opening gesture of autobiography |
| "He who knows how to breathe in the air of my writings is conscious that it is the air of the heights, that it is bracing. A man must be built for it, otherwise the chances are that it will chill him." | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 | Alpine metaphor for difficulty of his work |
| "The ice is near, the loneliness is terrible - but how serenely everything lies in the sunshine! how freely one can breathe! how much, one feels, lies beneath one!" | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 | Mountain solitude as privilege |
| "Philosophy, as I have understood it hitherto, is a voluntary retirement into regions of ice and mountain-peaks." | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 | Self-definition as alpine philosopher |
| "Error is not blindness; error is cowardice." | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 | Moral reformulation of epistemology |
| "I do not refute ideals; all I do is to draw on my gloves in their presence." | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 | Gentleman's contempt, elegant dismissal |
| "Nitimur in vetitum; with this device my philosophy will one day be victorious; for that which has hitherto been most stringently forbidden is, without exception, Truth." | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 | Latin motto - "We strive for the forbidden" |
| "The very last thing I should promise to accomplish would be to 'improve' mankind. I do not set up any new idols; may old idols only learn what it costs to have legs of clay." | Ecce Homo, Preface §2 | 1888 | Anti-reformist declaration |
| "The 'true world' and the 'apparent world' - in plain English, the fictitious world and reality." | Ecce Homo, Preface §2 | 1888 | Characteristic deflation through plain speech |
| "I live on my own self-made credit, and it is probably only a prejudice to suppose that I am alive at all." | Ecce Homo, Preface §1 | 1888 | Self-deprecating joke about obscurity |
| "It was during those years in which my vitality reached its lowest point that I ceased from being a pessimist: the instinct of self-recovery forbade my holding to a philosophy of poverty and desperation." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §2 | 1888 | Autobiographical philosophical turning point |
| "That which does not kill him makes him stronger. He instinctively gathers his material from all he sees, hears, and experiences. He is a selective principle; he rejects much." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §2 | 1888 | Self-portrait as selective organism |
| "He believes neither in 'ill-luck' nor 'guilt'; he can digest himself and others; he knows how to forget - he is strong enough to make everything turn to his own advantage." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §2 | 1888 | Self as healthy organism, anti-ressentiment |
| "Lo then! I am the very reverse of a decadent, for he whom I have just described is none other than myself." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §2 | 1888 | Triumphant self-identification |
| "I am gifted with a sense of cleanliness the keenness of which is phenomenal; so much so, that I can ascertain physiologically - that is to say, smell - the proximity, nay, the inmost core, the 'entrails' of every human soul." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §8 | 1888 | Claiming superhuman sensitivity |
| "My humanity is a perpetual process of self-mastery." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §8 | 1888 | Self-discipline as form of humanity |
| "The whole of my Zarathustra is a dithyramb in honour of solitude, or, if I have been understood, in honour of purity." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §8 | 1888 | Reframing solitude as achievement |
| "Why do I know more things than other people? Why, in fact, am I so clever? I have never pondered over questions that are not questions." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §1 | 1888 | Opening of self-analysis, mock-arrogance |
| "God is a too palpably clumsy solution of things; a solution which shows a lack of delicacy towards us thinkers - at bottom He is really no more than a coarse and rude prohibition of us: ye shall not think!" | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §1 | 1888 | God as intellectual insult |
| "I am much more interested in another question, - a question upon which the 'salvation of humanity' depends to a far greater degree than it does upon any piece of theological curiosity: I refer to nutrition." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §1 | 1888 | Deflating theology with physiology |
| "German intellect is indigestion; it can assimilate nothing." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §1 | 1888 | Cultural critique through digestive metaphor |
| "Remain seated as little as possible, put no trust in any thought that is not born in the open, to the accompaniment of free bodily motion - nor in one in which even the muscles do not celebrate a feast." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §1 | 1888 | Embodied philosophy, walking as method |
| "All prejudices take their origin in the intestines. A sedentary life, as I have already said elsewhere, is the real sin against the Holy Spirit." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §1 | 1888 | Body-philosophy, anti-desk polemic |
| "It was Heinrich Heine who gave me the most perfect idea of what a lyrical poet could be." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 | 1888 | Declaring literary kinship |
| "One day it will be said of Heine and me that we were by far the greatest artists of the German language that have ever existed." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 | 1888 | Self-ranking among literary masters |
| "After casting a glance between the pages of my Zarathustra, I pace my room to and fro for half an hour at a time, unable to overcome an insufferable fit of tears." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 | 1888 | Emotional overwhelm at own creation |
| "We all fear the truth." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 | 1888 | Sudden, stark confession |
| "I can do nought else. God help me! Amen." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Write Such Excellent Books" §2 | 1888 | Parodying Luther's "Here I stand" |
| "I have the smallest ears that have ever been seen. This fact is not without interest to women - it seems to me they feel that I understand them better!" | Ecce Homo, "Why I Write Such Excellent Books" §2 | 1888 | Flirtatious self-deprecation |
| "Old market-women cannot rest until they have picked out the sweetest of their grapes for me. To this extent must a man be a philosopher." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Write Such Excellent Books" §2 | 1888 | Comic image of philosopher in marketplace |
| "I am not successful at being pompous, the most I can do is to appear embarrassed." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Write Such Excellent Books" §2 | 1888 | Honest admission of social manner |
| "Non legor, non legar." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Write Such Excellent Books" §1 | 1888 | "I am not read, I shall not be read" - proud obscurity |
| "To have understood six sentences in that book - that is to say, to have lived them - raises a man to a higher level among mortals than 'modern' men can attain." | Ecce Homo, on Zarathustra | 1888 | Elitist standard for reading |
| "Life was easy - in fact easiest - to me, in those periods when it exacted the heaviest duties from me." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §10 | 1888 | Paradox of difficulty and ease |
| "Never have I eaten with more pleasant sensations, never has my sleep been better." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §10 | 1888 | Physical well-being during peak creativity |
| "I know of no other manner of dealing with great tasks, than as play." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §10 | 1888 | Greatness as lightness |
| "One must not have nerves. Even to suffer from solitude is an objection." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §10 | 1888 | Standard of strength |
| "A man who, after his four-and-fortieth year, can say that he has never bothered himself about honours, women, or money! - not that they did not come his way." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §9 | 1888 | Monk-like self-portrait with wink |
| "I am the very opposite in nature to the kind of man that has been honoured hitherto as virtuous." | Ecce Homo, Preface §2 | 1888 | Anti-virtue declaration |
| "In my lifework, my Zarathustra holds a place apart. With it, I gave my fellow-men the greatest gift that has ever been bestowed upon them." | Ecce Homo, Preface §4 | 1888 | Grandiose claim for his masterpiece |
| "They don't understand me: I am not the mouth for these ears." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue | 1883 | Zarathustra's alienation from the crowd |
| "Perhaps I have lived too long in the mountains; too long have I listened to the brooks and trees: now I speak to them as to the goatherds." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue | 1883 | Self-diagnosis of communicative failure |
| "There is ice in their laughter." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue | 1883 | Perceiving hostility beneath amusement |
| "Three metamorphoses of the spirit do I designate to you: how the spirit becometh a camel, the camel a lion, and the lion at last a child." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "The Three Metamorphoses" | 1883 | Central developmental schema |
| "I would believe only in a God that would know how to dance." | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "Of Reading and Writing" | 1883 | Dancing as divine criterion |
| "This is my morning, my day begins: rise up now, rise up, great noontide!" | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "The Sign" (closing words) | 1885 | Final exultant declaration |
| "Thus I invented, when I needed them, the 'free spirits' too, to whom this heavyhearted-stouthearted book is dedicated. There are no such 'free spirits,' were none - but I needed their company at the time." | Human, All Too Human, Preface §2 | 1886 | Confession of loneliness and self-consolation |
| "I needed their company to be of good cheer in the midst of bad things (illness, isolation, foreignness, sloth, inactivity); as brave fellows and specters to chat and laugh with, when one feels like chatting and laughing, and whom one sends to hell when they get boring." | Human, All Too Human, Preface §2 | 1886 | Charming description of imaginary companions |
| "I already see them coming, slowly, slowly." | Human, All Too Human, Preface §2 | 1886 | Prophetic patience, looking toward future |
| "Gratitude continually flows forth, as if the most unexpected thing had happened, the gratitude of a convalescent for convalescence was this most unexpected thing." | The Gay Science, Preface to 2nd edition | 1887 | Convalescence as philosophical metaphor |
| "'Joyful Wisdom': that implies the Saturnalia of a spirit which has patiently withstood a long, frightful pressure - patiently, strenuously, impassionately, without submitting, but without hope - and which is now suddenly overpowered with hope." | The Gay Science, Preface | 1887 | Self-description of intellectual recovery |
| "The whole book is really nothing but a revel after long privation and impotence: the frolicking of returning energy." | The Gay Science, Preface | 1887 | Exuberant self-characterization |
| "Maintaining cheerfulness in the midst of a gloomy task, fraught with immeasurable responsibility, is no small feat; and yet what is needed more than cheerfulness? Nothing succeeds if prankishness has no part in it." | Twilight of the Idols, Preface | 1888 | Programmatic gaiety in the face of gravity |
| "Excess strength alone is the proof of strength." | Twilight of the Idols, Preface | 1888 | Aphoristic definition |
| "There are more idols than realities in the world: that is my 'evil eye' upon this world; that is also my 'evil ear.'" | Twilight of the Idols, Preface | 1888 | Self as iconoclast |
| "I tame every bear, I can make even clowns behave decently." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §4 | 1888 | Claiming personal charm with comic swagger |
| "At heart I am a warrior. Attacking belongs to my instincts." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §7 | 1888 | Self-identification as combatant |
| "To be one's enemy's equal - this is the first condition of an honourable duel." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §7 | 1888 | Aristocratic code of intellectual warfare |
| "Those who keep silent are almost always lacking in subtlety and refinement of heart; silence is an objection, to swallow a grievance must necessarily produce a bad temper - it even upsets the stomach. All silent people are dyspeptic." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §5 | 1888 | Comic physiological psychology |
| "If a god were to descend to this earth, he would have to do nothing but wrong - to take guilt not punishment, on one's shoulders, is the first proof of divinity." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §5 | 1888 | Theological reversal, guilt as divine attribute |
| "I dispatch a pot of jam in order to get rid of a bitter experience." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §5 | 1888 | Comic metaphor for his revenge-by-kindness |
| "To this day I have never been able to detect any local degeneration in me." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §1 | 1888 | Medical self-analysis with wry pride |
| "A doctor who treated me for some time as a nerve patient finally declared: 'No! there is nothing wrong with your nerves, it is simply I who am nervous.'" | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §1 | 1888 | Self-mythologizing anecdote |
| "It is some times difficult, says Juvenal, to refrain from writing a satire." | Letter to his mother | June 1865 | Classical reference deployed for political satire |
| "I pass here among the students, etc., as something of an authority on music, and as a queer customer into the bargain." | Letter to his mother | February 1865 | Self-aware social portrait |
| "I am frequently unhappy and I have too many moods and am rather inclined to be a nagging spirit (Qutilgeist) not only to myself, but also to others." | Letter to his mother | February 1865 | Honest self-criticism at age 20 |
| "God forbid that I should write again in such a wooden, dry style and with so much logical tightlacing as I did in my Theognis essay, on the cradle of which none of the Graces ever lighted (on the contrary, it was more like the distant booming of the cannon at Koniggratz)." | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | April 1867 | Comic self-criticism of early writing |
| "Truth seldom resides in the temple men have built in her honour, or where priests have been ordained to her service." | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | April 1867 | Iconoclastic epistemology in a letter |
| "One must be one's own doctor and gather one's medical experience on one's own body." | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | April 1867 | Self-reliance doctrine |
| "Let us at least have the pleasure of scoring our blunders off our own bat." | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | April 1867 | Jovial British idiom in German letter |
| "I had resolved to have nothing to do with the Brahmans because they had philosophical training. Holy Ganges!" | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | April 1866 | Mocking a missionary's ignorance |
| "What did man with his restless will matter to me then? What did I care for the eternal 'Thou shalt' and 'Thou shalt not'? How different are lightning, storm and hail - free powers without ethics! How happy, how strong they are - pure will untrammeled by the muddling influence of the intellect!" | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | April 1866 | Ecstatic nature-worship at 21, proto-Zarathustra |

## Extended Passages

| Passage | Source | Date | Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| "The happiness of my existence, its unique character perhaps, consists in its fatefulness: to speak in a riddle, as my own father I am already dead, as my own mother I still live and grow old. This double origin, taken as it were from the highest and lowest rungs of the ladder of life, at once a decadent and a beginning, this, if anything, explains that neutrality, that freedom from partisanship in regard to the general problem of existence, which perhaps distinguishes me. To the first indications of ascending or of descending life my nostrils are more sensitive than those of any man that has yet lived. In this domain I am a master to my backbone - I know both sides, for I am both sides." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §1 | 1888 | Opening self-analysis: dual nature as decadent and beginning |
| "It is Saturday, and the day too is drawing to a close. For a soldier the word 'Saturday' is full of magic charm and of a feeling of quiet and peace of which as a student I had no idea. To be able to sleep and dream peacefully, without one's soul being taunted by the terrifying picture of the morrow; to have overcome and done with another seven days of that excitement in uniform which is called a year's soldiering - what simple and at the same time deep joys such things awaken - joys worthy of a cynic and attained by us almost too cheaply and easily. I now understand that first and greatest Saturday afternoon mood, in which that easy and satisfied phrase was pronounced; in which coffee and a pipe were invented, and the first optimist stepped into life." | Letter to Rohde | February 1868 | Lyrical military contentment, classical playfulness |
| "As it is my intention within a very short time to confront my fellow-men with the very greatest demand that has ever yet been made upon them, it seems to me above all necessary to declare here who and what I am. As a matter of fact, this ought to be pretty well known already, for I have not 'held my tongue' about myself. But the disparity which obtains between the greatness of my task and the smallness of my contemporaries, is revealed by the fact that people have neither heard me nor yet seen me. I live on my own self-made credit, and it is probably only a prejudice to suppose that I am alive at all." | Ecce Homo, Preface §1 | 1888 | Grand opening: the unrecognized prophet speaks |
| "To communicate a state, an inner tension of pathos by means of signs, including the tempo of these signs, - that is the meaning of every style; and in view of the fact that the multiplicity of inner states in me is enormous, I am capable of many kinds of style - in short, the most multifarious art of style that any man has ever had at his disposal. Any style is good which genuinely communicates an inner condition, which does not blunder over the signs, over the tempo of the signs, or over moods - all the laws of phrasing are the outcome of representing moods artistically." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Write Such Excellent Books" §4 | 1888 | Theory of style as physiological expression |
| "My present life, my dear friend, is really very lonely and friendless. It offers me no stimulation that I do not myself provide; none of that harmonious concord of souls which many a happy hour in Leipzig used to afford; but rather, enstrangement of the soul from itself, preponderance of obsessional influences, which draw the soul up tightly with a sense of fear, and teach it to regard things with an earnestness that they do not deserve. This is the seamy side of my present existence, and you will certainly be able to enter into my feelings about it." | Letter to Rohde | February 1868 | Intimate confession of military loneliness |
| "Thus I invented, when I needed them, the 'free spirits' too, to whom this heavyhearted-stouthearted book with the title 'Human, All Too Human' is dedicated. There are no such 'free spirits,' were none - but, as I said, I needed their company at the time, to be of good cheer in the midst of bad things (illness, isolation, foreignness, sloth, inactivity); as brave fellows and specters to chat and laugh with, when one feels like chatting and laughing, and whom one sends to hell when they get boring - as reparation for lacking friends. That there could someday be such free spirits, that our Europe will have such lively, daring fellows among its sons of tomorrow and the day after tomorrow, real and palpable and not merely, as in my case, phantoms and a hermit's shadow play: I am the last person to want to doubt that. I already see them coming, slowly, slowly." | Human, All Too Human, Preface §2 | 1886 | Confessional preface: loneliness, invention, hope |
| "So! And now candor 'unto death'! My dear friend! I am bound by nothing and change my plans with the greatest ease if you have plans. And if I should not be together with you, then simply tell me this too - and you don't even need to give reasons! I trust you completely: but you know that. If we are suited to each other, then our healths will also be suited to each other, and somewhere there will be a secret benefit. I have never thought until now that you should 'read aloud to me and write' for me; but I very much wished to be allowed to be your teacher. Finally, to tell the whole truth: I am now seeking people who could be my heirs; I carry some things around with me that absolutely cannot be read in my books - and am seeking for this the most beautiful and fruitful farmland. See my selfishness!" | Letter to Lou von Salome | May 1882 | Proposal of intellectual partnership - vulnerability and self-awareness |
| "'Joyful Wisdom': that implies the Saturnalia of a spirit which has patiently withstood a long, frightful pressure - patiently, strenuously, impassionately, without submitting, but without hope - and which is now suddenly overpowered with hope, the hope of health, the intoxication of convalescence. What wonder that much that is unreasonable and foolish thereby comes to light: much wanton tenderness expended even on problems which have a prickly hide, and are not therefore fit to be fondled and allured. The whole book is really nothing but a revel after long privation and impotence: the frolicking of returning energy, of newly awakened belief in a tomorrow and after-tomorrow; of sudden sentience and prescience of a future, of near adventures, of seas open once more, and aims once more permitted and believed in." | The Gay Science, Preface to 2nd edition | 1887 | Convalescent's exuberance, metaphor of intellectual recovery |
| "I condemn Christianity; I bring against the Christian church the most terrible of all the accusations that an accuser has ever had in his mouth. It is, to me, the greatest of all imaginable corruptions; it seeks to work the ultimate corruption, the worst possible corruption. The Christian church has left nothing untouched by its depravity; it has turned every value into worthlessness, and every truth into a lie, and every integrity into baseness of soul. Let any one dare to speak to me of its 'humanitarian' blessings! Its deepest necessities range it against any effort to abolish distress; it lives by distress; it creates distress to make itself immortal." | The Antichrist, §62 | 1888 | Climactic polemic - maximum rhetorical intensity |
| "I have never understood the art of arousing ill-feeling against myself, - this is also something for which I have to thank my incomparable father, - even when it seemed to me highly desirable to do so. However un-Christian it may seem, I do not even bear any ill-feeling towards myself. Turn my life about as you may, you will find but seldom - perhaps indeed only once - any trace of some one's having shown me ill-will. You might perhaps discover, however, too many traces of goodwill." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §4 | 1888 | Self-portrait: the non-resentful man |
| "For recreation I turn to three things, and a wonderful recreation they provide! my Schopenhauer, Schumann's music, and, finally, solitary walks. Yesterday a heavy storm hung in the sky, and I hastened up a neighbouring hill. On the summit I found a hut and a man killing two kids, with his son looking on. The storm broke with a mighty crash, discharging thunder and hail, and I felt inexpressibly well and full of zest, and realized with singular clearness that to understand Nature one must go to her as I had just done, as a refuge from all worries and oppressions. What did man with his restless will matter to me then? What did I care for the eternal 'Thou shalt' and 'Thou shalt not'? How different are lightning, storm and hail - free powers without ethics! How happy, how strong they are - pure will untrammeled by the muddling influence of the intellect!" | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | April 1866 | Proto-Nietzschean ecstasy: nature, will, freedom from morality at 21 |
| "Pious people believe that all the suffering and mishaps that come their way have been sent to them with the most careful premeditation. We lack the very first principles on which such a faith is based. It does lie in our power, however, to suck every event, every trivial or serious mishap, dry and to turn it to account for our improvement and discipline. The predestined character of every individual's fate is no myth if we understand it in this sense. What we have to do is intentionally to turn our fate to account, for events are, in themselves, but insignificant accessories to this end. It all turns upon our personal attitude." | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | February 1867 | Early amor fati in letter form |
| "At heart I am a warrior. Attacking belongs to my instincts. To be able to be an enemy, to be an enemy - maybe these things presuppose a strong nature; in any case all strong natures involve these things. Such natures need resistance, consequently they go in search of obstacles: the pathos of aggression belongs of necessity to strength as much as the feelings of revenge and of rancour belong to weakness. The strength of the aggressor can be measured by the opposition which he needs; every increase of growth betrays itself by a seeking out of more formidable opponents - or problems: for a philosopher who is combative challenges even problems to a duel." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §7 | 1888 | Warrior-philosopher self-definition |
| "Freedom from resentment and the understanding of the nature of resentment - who knows how very much after all I am indebted to my long illness for these two things? To be ill is a sort of resentment in itself. Against this resentment the invalid has only one great remedy - I call it Russian fatalism, that fatalism which is free from revolt, and with which the Russian soldier, to whom a campaign proves unbearable, ultimately lays himself down in the snow. To accept nothing more, to undertake nothing more, to absorb nothing more - to cease entirely from reacting. The tremendous sagacity of this fatalism, which does not always imply merely the courage for death, but which in the most dangerous cases may actually constitute a self-preservative measure, amounts to a reduction of activity in the vital functions." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §6 | 1888 | Russian fatalism as survival strategy - illness philosophy |
| "Maintaining cheerfulness in the midst of a gloomy task, fraught with immeasurable responsibility, is no small feat; and yet what is needed more than cheerfulness? Nothing succeeds if prankishness has no part in it. Excess strength alone is the proof of strength. A revaluation of all values, this question mark, so black, so huge that it casts a shadow over him who sets it up - such a destiny of a task compels one every instant to run out into the sunshine so as to shake off a seriousness that has become crushing, far too crushing. Every expedient for doing this is justifiable, every 'event' is an expedient. Above all, war. War has always been the great policy of all spirits who have penetrated too far into themselves or who have grown too profound; even a wound has curative powers." | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am a Fatality" | 1888 | Cheerfulness as philosophical program, war as therapy |
| "The genius of the heart, as that great anchorite possesses it, the divine tempter and born Pied Piper of consciences, whose voice knows how to sink into the inmost depths of every soul, who neither utters a word nor casts a glance, in which some seductive motive or trick does not lie. The genius of the heart which makes all loud and self-conceited things hold their tongues and lend their ears, which polishes all rough souls and makes them taste a new longing - to lie placid as a mirror, that the deep heavens may be reflected in them. The genius of the heart, from contact with which every man goes away richer, not 'blessed' and overcome, but richer in himself, fresher to himself than before, opened up, breathed upon and sounded by a thawing wind." | Beyond Good and Evil, §295 (quoted in Ecce Homo) | 1886 | Self-portrait as Dionysus, seducer of souls |
| "Now and again one enjoys hours of peaceful reflection when, with mingled gladness and sorrow, one seems to hover over one's life just as those lovely summer days seem to lie stretched out at ease above the hilltops. It is then that Nature is perfect, and we feel the same; then we are free from the spell of the ever vigilant will; then we are nothing but a pure, contemplative and dispassionate eye. It is in a mood such as this - a mood desirable above all others - that I take up my pen to reply to your kind and thoughtful letter." | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | April 1866 | Lyrical meditation, Schopenhauerian mood at 21 |

## Signature Phrases

| Phrase | Trigger | Frequency | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| "amor fati" | When affirming his fate, suffering, or life in its totality | Very frequent (dozens) | Ecce Homo, Gay Science, letters |
| "will to power" (Wille zur Macht) | When explaining the fundamental drive behind life, knowledge, morality | Pervasive (hundreds) | Beyond Good and Evil, Zarathustra, Nachlass |
| "eternal recurrence" (ewige Wiederkehr) | When posing the ultimate test of life-affirmation | Central doctrine, dozens | Gay Science §341, Zarathustra |
| "beyond good and evil" (jenseits von Gut und Bose) | When transcending or critiquing moral categories | Pervasive | Beyond Good and Evil, Zarathustra |
| "Ubermensch" / "Superman" / "Overman" | When describing what humanity should aspire toward | Central, dozens | Thus Spoke Zarathustra |
| "herd morality" / "slave morality" / "master morality" | When critiquing democratic, Christian, or egalitarian values | Very frequent | Genealogy of Morals, BGE |
| "transvaluation of all values" (Umwerthung aller Werthe) | When describing his ultimate philosophical project | Frequent, especially late works | Ecce Homo, Antichrist, letters |
| "we free spirits" (wir freien Geister) | When addressing his ideal audience, fellow iconoclasts | Recurring motif | Human All Too Human, BGE, Gay Science |
| "God is dead" | When diagnosing the collapse of metaphysical foundations | Several key passages | Gay Science §125, Zarathustra |
| "Thus spoke Zarathustra" | As closing refrain after prophetic discourse | Dozens (structural device) | Thus Spoke Zarathustra |
| "the great noontide" (der grosse Mittag) | When prophesying humanity's decisive moment | Recurring | Zarathustra, Ecce Homo |
| "become what you are" (werde, der du bist) | When affirming self-realization through fate | Subtitle of Ecce Homo, recurrent | Ecce Homo, Gay Science |
| "this is my morning, my day begins" | When declaring a new beginning or triumph | Climactic, several | Zarathustra closing |
| the dash ( - ) | As rhythmic interruption, change of pace, interjection | Omnipresent stylistic tic | All works |
| the exclamation mark (!) | For emphasis, urgency, mock-heroic pitch | Extremely frequent | All works |
| "in plain English" / "in short" / "to sum up shortly" | When deflating an abstraction into blunt speech | Recurring rhetorical device | Ecce Homo, BGE |
| "between ourselves" (unter uns) | Creating intimacy with reader, sharing a confidence | Frequent in letters and books | Letters, Ecce Homo, BGE |
| "old man" (alter Freund) | When addressing close male friends | Very frequent in letters | Letters to Gersdorff, Rohde |
| "my dear friend" (mein lieber Freund) | Opening salutation in correspondence | Standard in letters | Letters to all major correspondents |
| "I have no doubt" / "who doubts" | When asserting something provocative as certain | Recurring assertive device | All works |
| "I was the first" | When claiming priority of insight | Frequent in Ecce Homo | Ecce Homo throughout |
| "that is to say" | Explanatory interjection, mid-sentence gloss | Very frequent stylistic habit | All works |
| "let us" / "let me" | Inclusive imperative, drawing reader along | Very frequent | All works |
| "a symptom of decadence" | When diagnosing cultural illness | Recurring diagnostic phrase | Ecce Homo, Twilight, Antichrist |
| "the instinct of" | When explaining behavior through drives | Very frequent | All middle and late works |
| "dangerous" / "dangerously" | When praising courage, risk, or ideas that threaten comfort | Extremely frequent | Gay Science, BGE, Ecce Homo |
| "What? Art thou..." / "Thou seekest..." | When using archaic address for prophetic register | Zarathustra, poems | Zarathustra, Twilight |
| "Verily" (Wahrlich) | Prophetic affirmation, Zarathustra voice | Pervasive in Zarathustra | Thus Spoke Zarathustra |
| "pathos of distance" | When describing the feeling of rank-difference | Key concept, recurring | BGE, Genealogy |
| "I attack only..." | When laying out his war principles | Ecce Homo specifically | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §7 |
| "where one commands, where one sees something beneath one, one ought not to wage war" | Code of intellectual honor | Key passage | Ecce Homo |
| "the whole of" | Characteristic totalizing phrase | Very frequent | All works |
| "precisely" (gerade) | Emphatic qualifier | Extremely frequent | All works |
| "the question is" / "the problem is" | When reframing an issue on his terms | Recurring | All philosophical works |
| "nothing on earth" / "nothing in the world" | Absolute emphasis | Frequent | All works |

## Register Samples

| Sample | Medium | Emotion | Source | Date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "Dear Elizabeth: As mother is writing to you to-day I am sending you a short note to put with hers. First of all, let me describe our journey. On the way to Weissenfels there was nothing I objected to more than the piercing wind." | written-informal | neutral | Letter to his sister | March 1856 |
| "Dear Mother: I am very sorry that I was not able to meet you at Almrich yesterday, but I was prevented from coming by being kept in. And thereby hangs a tale which I will tell you." | written-informal | defensive | Letter to his mother | November 1862 |
| "Dear Mother: If I write to you to-day it is certainly about the saddest and most unpleasant business that it has ever been my lot to relate. For I have been very wicked and do not know whether you will or can forgive me." | written-informal | sad | Letter to his mother | April 1863 |
| "I condemn Christianity; I bring against the Christian church the most terrible of all the accusations that an accuser has ever had in his mouth. It is, to me, the greatest of all imaginable corruptions; it seeks to work the ultimate corruption, the worst possible corruption." | written-formal | angry | The Antichrist, §62 | 1888 |
| "On this perfect day, when everything is ripening, and not only the grapes are getting brown, a ray of sunshine has fallen on my life: I looked behind me, I looked before me, and never have I seen so many good things all at once." | written-formal | excited | Ecce Homo, Preface | 1888 |
| "My dear friend, yesterday my sister wrote to tell me that she would like to use my 'right' to a seat in Bayreuth. I have truly not become a 'monster'!" | written-informal | defensive | Letter to Franz Overbeck | 1882 |
| "So! And now candor 'unto death'! My dear friend! I am bound by nothing and change my plans with the greatest ease if you have plans." | written-informal | excited | Letter to Lou von Salome | May 1882 |
| "My present life, my dear friend, is really very lonely and friendless. It offers me no stimulation that I do not myself provide." | written-informal | sad | Letter to Rohde | February 1868 |
| "Old market-women cannot rest until they have picked out the sweetest of their grapes for me. To this extent must a man be a philosopher." | written-formal | humorous | Ecce Homo | 1888 |
| "I am a bombardier in the second mounted division of the Fourth Horse Artillery. You may well imagine how astonished I was by this revolution in my affairs." | written-informal | humorous | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | December 1867 |
| "Really these dissertations on Christianity and on opinions of this man and that as to what I should do and ought to think on the subject should no longer be directed to my address. My patience won't stand it!" | written-informal | angry | Letter to his mother | c. 1880s |
| "To Princess Ariadne, My Beloved. It is a mere prejudice that I am a human being. Yet I have often enough dwelled among human beings. Among the Hindus I was Buddha, in Greece Dionysus - Alexander and Caesar were incarnations of me, as well as the poet of Shakespeare, Lord Bacon." | written-informal | excited | Letter to Cosima Wagner (madness letter) | January 3, 1889 |
| "I teach you the Superman. Man is something that should be overcome. What have you done to overcome him? All creatures hitherto have created something beyond themselves: and do you want to be the ebb of this great tide?" | written-formal | excited | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Prologue | 1883 |
| "It was during those years in which my vitality reached its lowest point that I ceased from being a pessimist: the instinct of self-recovery forbade my holding to a philosophy of poverty and desperation." | written-formal | contemplative | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Wise" §2 | 1888 |
| "Ah, my dear friend, what a child of misfortune is a field artilleryman when he has literary tastes into the bargain. Our old god of War loved young women, not shrivelled old Muses." | written-informal | humorous | Letter to Rohde | February 1868 |
| "He who knows how to breathe in the air of my writings is conscious that it is the air of the heights, that it is bracing. A man must be built for it, otherwise the chances are that it will chill him. The ice is near, the loneliness is terrible - but how serenely everything lies in the sunshine!" | written-formal | contemplative | Ecce Homo, Preface §3 | 1888 |
| "Wherever Germany extends her sway, she ruins culture. It was the war which first saved the spirit of France." | written-formal | angry | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §3 | 1888 |
| "I know not how to draw any distinction between tears and music. I do not know how to think either of joy, or of the south, without a shudder of fear." | written-formal | sad | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §7 | 1888 |
| "We all fear the truth." | written-formal | contemplative | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 | 1888 |
| "One day it will be said of Heine and me that we were by far the greatest artists of the German language that have ever existed, and that we left all the efforts that mere Germans made in this language an incalculable distance behind us." | written-formal | excited | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 | 1888 |
| "After casting a glance between the pages of my Zarathustra, I pace my room to and fro for half an hour at a time, unable to overcome an insufferable fit of tears." | written-formal | sad | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §4 | 1888 |
| "I am not successful at being pompous, the most I can do is to appear embarrassed." | written-formal | humorous | Ecce Homo, "Why I Write Such Excellent Books" §2 | 1888 |
| "German politics are only another form of permanent winter and bad weather. It seems to me that Germany for the last 15 years has become a regular school of besotment." | written-informal | angry | Letter from Nice | February 1887 |
| "If only she could learn to write a little better! When she is describing anything she must try and avoid all those 'Ahs!' and 'Ohs!' 'You cannot imagine how magnificent, how marvellous, how bewitching, etc., it was' - she must drop this sort of thing." | written-informal | affectionate | Letter to his mother about Elizabeth | February 1862 |
| "I know of no other manner of dealing with great tasks, than as play: this, as a sign of greatness, is an essential prerequisite. The slightest constraint, a sombre mien, any hard accent in the voice - all these things are objections to a man, but how much more to his work!" | written-formal | contemplative | Ecce Homo, "Why I Am So Clever" §10 | 1888 |
| "Verily, a strong wind is Zarathustra to all low lands; and this is his counsel to his foes and to all those who spit and spew: 'Beware of spitting against the wind!'" | written-formal | humorous | Thus Spoke Zarathustra | 1883 |
| "The beauty of the Superman came to me as a shadow. Ah, my brothers! What are the gods to me now!" | written-formal | excited | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, "On the Blissful Islands" | 1883 |
| "This is my morning, my day begins: rise up now, rise up, great noontide!" | written-formal | excited | Thus Spoke Zarathustra, closing | 1885 |
| "Yesterday a heavy storm hung in the sky, and I hastened up a neighbouring hill. The storm broke with a mighty crash, discharging thunder and hail, and I felt inexpressibly well and full of zest." | written-informal | excited | Letter to Karl von Gersdorff | April 1866 |
| "I was in Rosenthal, where Carmen-music lured me. I drank three glasses of water and the first (ha! how ugly it tasted!) and thought in all innocence and malice whether I might not have some disposition toward madness. I said no in the end. Then the Carmen-music began, and I was for half an hour submerged in tears and heart-pounding." | written-informal | contemplative | Letter to Lou von Salome | September 1882 |

## Humor Samples

| Sample | Type | Context | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Idleness is the parent of all psychology. What? Is psychology then a - vice?" | Self-referential wit | Opening Twilight of the Idols with mock alarm at his own profession | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims #1 |
| "Man does not aspire to happiness; only the Englishman does that." | National satire | Deflating utilitarianism with a jab at English philosophy | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims #12 |
| "Which is it? Is man only a blunder of God? Or is God only a blunder of man?" | Reversal | Theological speculation turned into a symmetrical joke | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims #7 |
| "When stepped on, a worm doubles up. That is clever. In that way he lessens the probability of being stepped on again. In the language of morality: humility." | Satirical redescription | Reducing Christian virtue to animal reflex | Twilight of the Idols, Maxims #31 |
| "Around the hero everything becomes a tragedy, around the demigod a satyr-play; and around God - what? perhaps a 'world'?" | Ascending absurdity | Building through mythological registers to cosmological punchline | Beyond Good and Evil, §150 |
| "Old market-women cannot rest until they have picked out the sweetest of their grapes for me. To this extent must a man be a philosopher." | Mock-grandiosity | Claiming market-women's affection as proof of philosophical status | Ecce Homo |
| "I am not successful at being pompous, the most I can do is to appear embarrassed." | Self-deprecation | Honest admission undercutting the book's grandiose tone | Ecce Homo |
| "A doctor who treated me for some time as a nerve patient finally declared: 'No! there is nothing wrong with your nerves, it is simply I who am nervous.'" | Anecdote/punchline | Doctor confesses his own nervousness before Nietzsche's calm | Ecce Homo |
| "I dispatch a pot of jam in order to get rid of a bitter experience." | Extended metaphor | Explaining his revenge-by-generosity through comic domestic image | Ecce Homo |
| "German intellect is indigestion; it can assimilate nothing." | National satire | Reducing German philosophical tradition to gastrointestinal complaint | Ecce Homo |
| "God is a too palpably clumsy solution of things; a solution which shows a lack of delicacy towards us thinkers - at bottom He is really no more than a coarse and rude prohibition of us: ye shall not think!" | Comic indignation | Treating God as a social insult, an affront to intellectual manners | Ecce Homo |
| "I am much more interested in another question - a question upon which the 'salvation of humanity' depends to a far greater degree than it does upon any piece of theological curiosity: I refer to nutrition." | Deflation | Sudden pivot from cosmic to mundane, theology to diet | Ecce Homo |
| "God's only excuse is that He does not exist." | One-liner | Attributed by Nietzsche to Stendhal, claimed as something he would have said | Ecce Homo |
| "Non legor, non legar." | Latin inversion | Inverting Schopenhauer's proud "I shall be read" into "I am not read, shall not be read" | Ecce Homo |
| "I have the smallest ears that have ever been seen. This fact is not without interest to women - it seems to me they feel that I understand them better!" | Flirtatious non-sequitur | Random physical detail spun into sexual innuendo | Ecce Homo |
| "It is some times difficult, says Juvenal, to refrain from writing a satire." | Classical quotation deployed | Watching pompous student festivities, barely containing mockery | Letter to his mother, 1865 |
| "Our old god of War loved young women, not shrivelled old Muses. A gunner who often enough in his barrack room sits upon a dirty stool meditating upon Democritean problems, while his boots are being polished for him, is really a paradox on whom the gods must look with scorn." | Extended comic image | Self-portrait as absurd military-intellectual hybrid | Letter to Rohde, 1868 |
| "They don't understand me: I am not the mouth for these ears. Perhaps I have lived too long in the mountains; too long have I listened to the brooks and trees: now I speak to them as to the goatherds. And now do they look at me and laugh: and while they laugh they hate me too. There is ice in their laughter." | Zarathustra's comic pathos | The prophet discovers his audience thinks he's a clown | Zarathustra, Prologue |
| "'Give us this last man, O Zarathustra' - they called out - 'Make us into these last men! Then will we make you a present of the overman!' And all the people laughed and clucked with their tongues." | Crowd comedy | Zarathustra's ideal is mistaken for a joke; they demand the opposite | Zarathustra, Prologue |
| "To call the taming of an animal its 'improvement' sounds almost like a joke to our ears. Whoever knows what goes on in kennels doubts that dogs are 'improved' there." | Satirical comparison | Likening Christian morality to kennel-training | Twilight of the Idols |
| "Supposing that Truth is a woman - what then? Is there not ground for suspecting that all philosophers, in so far as they have been dogmatists, have failed to understand women?" | Playful provocation | Opening Beyond Good and Evil by gendering Truth | Beyond Good and Evil, Preface |
| "The stern masters, who were very much surprised that anyone should introduce humour into so solemn an undertaking, summoned me to attend the Synod." | Comic understatement | Teenage Nietzsche punished for writing a school inspection report as jokes | Letter to his mother, 1862 |
| "I can do nought else. God help me! Amen." | Parody | Parodying Luther's "Here I stand, I can do no other" | Ecce Homo |
| "If a man wish to get rid of a feeling of insufferable oppression, he has to take to hashish. Well, I had to take to Wagner." | Comic analogy | Comparing Wagner's music to drug use for spiritual survival | Ecce Homo |
| "I go everywhere in my student coat, now and then slap someone on the back, and say: siamo contenti? son dio, ho fatto questa caricatura." | Absurdist comedy | Madness letter - claiming to be God while slapping strangers | Letter to Burckhardt, January 1889 |
| "A joke is an epigram on the death of a feeling." | Definition as aphorism | Defining humor through its destructive function | Attributed/Nachlass |
| "I would allow myself to order the ranks of philosophers according to the rank of their laughter - right up to those who are capable of golden laughter." | Philosophical ranking | Laughter as the true hierarchy of thinkers | Beyond Good and Evil, §294 |
| "Assuming that the gods also practise philosophy - I don't doubt that in the process they know how to laugh in a superhuman and new way - and at the expense of all serious things! Gods delight in making fun: even where sacred actions are concerned, it seems they cannot stop laughing." | Divine comedy | Gods as laughing philosophers; sacredness as comedy | Beyond Good and Evil, §294 |
